ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 8, 10, 40459, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

- `position` text NULL

- `sprache` text NULL

- `volumen` double precision NULL
- `waehrung` text NULL

- `Website` text

- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text

- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.

- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 8, 10, 40459, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.


## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.ansprechpartner  (201 rows)

Columns:
- `ap_id` text NOT NULL
- `vorname` text NULL
- `nachname` text NULL
- `email_adresse` text NULL
- `telefonnummer` text NULL
- `position` text NULL
- `funktion` text NULL
- `sprache` text NULL
- `kunde` text NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.assets  (400 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_id` text NOT NULL
- `bezeichnung` text NULL
- `seriennr` text NULL
- `garantie_bis` text NULL
- `kd_ref` text NULL
- `projekt_ref` text NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.chancen  (235 rows)

Columns:
- `chance_id` text NOT NULL
- `bezeichnung` text NULL
- `phase` text NULL
- `abschlussdatum` text NULL
- `volumen` double precision NULL
- `waehrung` text NULL
- `kd_nr` text NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.kunden  (100 rows)

Columns:
- `kunden_nr` text NOT NULL
- `firmenname` text NULL
- `erp_nummer` text NULL
- `kategorie` text NULL
- `gebiet` text NULL
- `branche` text NULL
- `webseite` text NULL
- `ort` text NULL
- `land` text NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.proj  (155 rows)

Columns:
- `proj_id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `kd` text NULL
- `opp` text NULL

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)

## Contact
Columns:

- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:

- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: after writing models, call run_dbt(), fix any errors, and read back every model file you edit before finishing.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 8, 10, 40468, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 8, 10, 40839, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62ac-5937-719f-82e5-3c12a634e402', conversation_id='019f62ac-5937-719f-82e5-3c113837a90f')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='Let me analyze the source data and target schema to create the dbt models. I need to understand the mapping between source columns and target columns, identify key transformations needed, and then write 5 model files plus a sources.yml file.

Let me first introspect the source data to understand the actual values and formats, then build the models.

Source schemas:
- `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.ansprechpartner` - contacts/contacts (ap_id, vorname, nachname, email_adresse, telefonnummer, position, funktion, sprache, kunde)
- `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.assets` - assets (asset_id, bezeichnung, seriennr, garantie_bis, kd_ref, projekt_ref)
- `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.chancen` - opportunities (chance_id, bezeichnung, phase, abschlussdatum, volumen, waehrung, kd_nr)
- `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.kunden` - accounts/customers (kunden_nr, firmenname, erp_nummer, kategorie, gebiet, branche, webseite, ort, land)
- `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.proj` - projects (proj_id, name, status, go_live, kd, opp)

Target tables:
1. Account - from kunden
2. Contact - from ansprechpartner
3. Opportunity - from chancen

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 8, 10, 40459, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.


## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.ansprechpartner  (201 rows)

Columns:
- `ap_id` text NOT NULL
- `vorname` text NULL
- `nachname` text NULL
- `email_adresse` text NULL
- `telefonnummer` text NULL
- `position` text NULL
- `funktion` text NULL
- `sprache` text NULL
- `kunde` text NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.assets  (400 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_id` text NOT NULL
- `bezeichnung` text NULL
- `seriennr` text NULL
- `garantie_bis` text NULL
- `kd_ref` text NULL
- `projekt_ref` text NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.chancen  (235 rows)

Columns:
- `chance_id` text NOT NULL
- `bezeichnung` text NULL
- `phase` text NULL
- `abschlussdatum` text NULL
- `volumen` double precision NULL
- `waehrung` text NULL
- `kd_nr` text NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.kunden  (100 rows)

Columns:
- `kunden_nr` text NOT NULL
- `firmenname` text NULL
- `erp_nummer` text NULL
- `kategorie` text NULL
- `gebiet` text NULL
- `branche` text NULL
- `webseite` text NULL
- `ort` text NULL
- `land` text NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.proj  (155 rows)

Columns:
- `proj_id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `kd` text NULL
- `opp` text NULL

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: after writing models, call run_dbt(), fix any errors, and read back every model file you edit before finishing.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 8, 10, 40468, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 8, 10, 40839, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62ac-5937-719f-82e5-3c12a634e402', conversation_id='019f62ac-5937-719f-82e5-3c113837a90f')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='The query_postgres tool is not available, so I'll work directly with what I know from the source and target schemas. Let me write the dbt models based on the transformation guidelines provided.

Let me map each source table to its target:

1. **Account** ← `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.kunden`
   - customers: kunden_nr, firmenname, erp_nummer, kategorie, gebiet, branche, webseite, ort, land
   - Account: Id, Name, ERP_Number__c, Customer_Tier__c, Region__c, Industry, Website, BillingCity, BillingCountry, Legacy_Customer_ID__c, CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate, IsDeleted

2. **Contact** ← `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.ansprechpartner`
   - contacts: ap_id, vorname, nachname, email_adresse, telefonnummer, position, funktion, sprache, kunde
   - Contact: Id, FirstName, LastName, Email, Phone, Title, Role__c, Preferred_Language__c, AccountId, Legacy_Contact_ID__c, CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate, IsDeleted

3. **Opportunity** ← `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.chancen`
   - opportunities: chance_id, bezeichnung, phase, abschlussdatum, volumen, waehrung, kd_nr
   - Opportunity: Id, Name, StageName, CloseDate, Amount, CurrencyIsoCode, AccountId, Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c, CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate, IsDeleted

4. **Project__c** ← `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.proj`
   - projects: proj_id, name, status, go_live, kd, opp
   - Project__c: Id, Name, Project_Status__c, Go_Live_Date__c, Account__c, Opportunity__c, Legacy_Project_ID__c, CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate, IsDeleted

5. **Installed_Asset__c** ← `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.assets`
   - assets: asset_id, bezeichnung, seriennr, garantie_bis, kd_ref, projekt_ref
   - Installed_Asset__c: Id, Name, Serial_Number__c, Warranty_End_Date__c, Account__c, Project__c, Legacy_Asset_ID__c, CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate, IsDeleted

Key mapping considerations:
- Source keys to Salesforce IDs: I need to figure out how source customer numbers map to Salesforce-style Account Ids. Since these are raw source tables without Salesforce IDs, the "Id" columns in target will likely be a transformed version of the source natural keys (e.g., prefixed with '001' for Account which is standard Salesforce format).
- Actually, looking more carefully at the rules: "AccountId and Account__c must reference the Salesforce-style Account Id, not raw source customer numbers." So I should produce consistent key transformations across all models using source keys directly, then when dbt runs with materialized tables, the joins would work. But since we can't use ref(), each model independently needs to transform the source key into a consistent Salesforce-style ID format.

For Salesforce-style IDs:
- Account Ids typically start with '001'
- Contact Ids typically start with '003'  
- Opportunity Ids typically start with '006'
- Custom object record ids like Project__c and Installed_Asset__c would have their own prefixes

But the source doesn't have Salesforce IDs. So I need to generate them from source keys. A common approach is to hash or concatenate a prefix with the source key. Since this is a fixture, there might be a simple pattern.

Looking at the data more carefully:

- Source kd_nr (in chancen): text - references customers  
- Source kd (in proj): text - references customers
- Source kd_ref (in assets): text - references customers

All these "kd" prefixes likely mean "Kunde" (German for customer). So source customer numbers are referenced as kunde, kd_nr, kd, kd_ref across different tables.

For the Account Id, I could use something like a hash or simply format it. Since this is a test fixture, let me think about what makes sense... 

Actually, I think the simplest approach is to generate Salesforce-style IDs by prefixing the source key with the appropriate SObject prefix:
- Account: '001' || MD5 or similar... but that might be overcomplicating.

Wait - looking at this more carefully, since these are raw source tables and we're producing a dbt transformation layer, the "Id" columns in the target probably just need to be unique identifiers. They could be simple hashes or formatted versions of source keys.

Let me think about this differently. In a real scenario, you'd have Salesforce IDs already. But here, since it's a transformation from non-Salesforce sources, I'll generate consistent IDs by:
- Using a deterministic hash/prefix combination of the source natural key
- Or simply using '001' prefix for Account Ids based on the customer number

Since I can't query to check, let me use a reasonable approach and see what happens when dbt runs:
- Account Id: `CONCAT('001', SUBSTR(MD5(kunden_nr), 1, 10))` or just `'001' || kunden_nr` 
- Actually, for simplicity and consistency, let me use: `CONCAT('001', SUBSTR(ENCODE(MD5(kunden_nr::bytea), 'hex'), 1, 17))` which gives a Salesforce-style Account ID

Hmm, this is getting complex. Let me simplify - perhaps just using the source key directly as the Id, with appropriate prefix for Salesforce style:
- Account.Id = '001' || SUBSTR(MD5(kunden_nr::text), 1, 17) — gives exactly 20 chars like Salesforce IDs

Actually, you know what, let me just try a simpler approach first. Maybe the expected behavior is to use something straightforward like:
- Account.Id = 'A' || kunden_nr (for example if kunden_nr is numeric)

Or even simpler: The source keys might already be in a format that works directly. Let me just write the models with reasonable transformations and see what happens when I run dbt.

For IDs, I'll generate Salesforce-style IDs using MD5 hash prefix approach since these are new records being created in the target (no existing Salesforce IDs to reference):
- Account.Id = CONCAT('001', LEFT(ENCODE(MD5(kunden_nr::BYTEA), 'HEX'), 17))

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 11, 1, 636201, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

Errors:
overall_status: partial
- contact: Database Error in model contact (models/contact.sql)
  syntax error at or near "DE"
  LINE 28: ... WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('DE', 'GERMAN', 'D) THEN 'DE'
                                                                       ^
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/contact.sql
- opportunity: Database Error in model opportunity (models/opportunity.sql)
  invalid input syntax for type double precision: ""
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/opportunity.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 11, 1, 636209, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 11, 1, 636491, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62ae-f783-70c0-9d9a-f2bd3cdb7f9e', conversation_id='019f62ae-f783-70c0-9d9a-f2bc1693a26b')

1. `contact` model - syntax error at or near "DE" on LINE 28. It seems like there's a quoting issue in the SQL where 'D' appears to be cut off. The line shows `'D)` which suggests a single quote was not properly closed or escaped somewhere, causing the parser to misinterpret the string literal.

2. `opportunity` model - invalid input syntax for type double precision: "". This means some field is trying to cast an empty string to double precision.

Let me first read both failing models to see what's wrong.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/contact.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_y2n964qe'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/opportunity.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_vbg759rd')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=1691, output_tokens=187), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 11, 16, 22335, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 11, 16, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-ef09ee14-972b-4aca-b7ba-21d25cde7b92', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='Let me analyze the errors:

1. **contact model**: Syntax error at or near "DE" on LINE 28. Looking at the code:
```sql
WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('DE', 'GERMAN', 'D) THEN 'DE'
```
There's a missing closing single quote after `'D`. It should be `'DE'` not `'D)`. This is clearly a typo - the string `'DE'` got cut off.

2. **opportunity model**: invalid input syntax for type double precision: "". This means `waehrung` can be an empty string, and when it goes through the CASE statement, the empty string doesn't match any of the patterns (like `'^[.,\d-]+$'`) properly, or it falls through to a branch where it tries to cast an empty string.

Let me look more carefully at the opportunity model's Amount logic:
- `WHEN waehrung ~ '^[.,\d-]+$'` - this pattern matches strings with only dots, commas, digits, and dashes. An empty string would NOT match this because of the `+` which requires at least one character.
- So an empty string would fall through to the ELSE branch: `CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)` - which would give `''::DOUBLE PRECISION` and fail.

I need to handle empty strings in the opportunity model. Let me add a check for empty strings or NULLs first.

Let me also look at the contact model again:
```sql
WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('DE', 'GERMAN', 'D) THEN 'DE'
```
This should be:
```sql
WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('DE', 'GERMAN') THEN 'DE'
```

Now let me also check if there are any other issues. Let me fix both models.

For the contact model, I just need to fix the typo on line 28.

For the opportunity model, I need to add handling for empty strings in the Amount calculation. I should handle the case where waehrung is NULL or empty string.

Let me also consider: if `waehrung` is an empty string, REGEXP_REPLACE would return empty string, and casting '' to DOUBLE PRECISION fails.

I'll fix both issues now.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content='I see two issues:

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WAHRNEHMUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PERCEPTION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANGEBOT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSAL%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PREIS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRICE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ZITAT%' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERHANDLUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEGOTIAT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRÜFUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%REVIEW%' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS GEWONN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED WON%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%GEWONNEN%' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS VERLOREN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED LOST%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERLOREN%' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN waehrung ~ '^[.,\d-]+$' THEN CAST(waehrung AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,.]+', '', 'g'), '^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$', '\1\2.\3')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND NOT waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.]+' , '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ ',' AND NOT waehrung ~ '\.' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,]+', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('USD', 'EURO', 'EUR', '€', '$') THEN 'USD'
        ELSE LEFT(UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)), 3)
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    CONCAT('001', LEFT(MD5(kd_nr), 17)) AS "AccountId",
    chance_id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'chancen') }}

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.

        ELSE 'End User'

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('EN', 'ENGLISH') THEN 'EN'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('FR', 'FRENCH') THEN 'FR'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('ES', 'SPANISH') THEN 'ES'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('IT', 'ITALIAN') THEN 'IT'

    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'ansprechpartner') }}

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%QUALIFIZIERUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%QUALIFICAT%' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%BEDARFS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEEDS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WERT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VALUE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSITION%' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ENTSCHEID%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%DECISION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%MASTERS%' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WAHRNEHMUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PERCEPTION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANGEBOT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSAL%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PREIS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRICE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ZITAT%' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERHANDLUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEGOTIAT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRÜFUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%REVIEW%' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS VERLOREN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED LOST%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERLOREN%' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN waehrung ~ '^[.,\d-]+$' THEN CAST(waehrung AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,.]+', '', 'g'), '^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$', '\1\2.\3')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND NOT waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.]+' , '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ ',' AND NOT waehrung ~ '\.' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,]+', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('USD', 'EURO', 'EUR', '€', '$') THEN 'USD'
        ELSE LEFT(UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)), 3)
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    CONCAT('001', LEFT(MD5(kd_nr), 17)) AS "AccountId",
    chance_id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'chancen') }}

2. **opportunity model**: invalid input syntax for type double precision: "". Let me look more carefully at the opportunity model's Amount logic:
- `WHEN waehrung ~ '^[.,\d-]+$'` - this pattern matches strings with only dots, commas, digits, and dashes. An empty string would NOT match this because of the `+` which requires at least one character.
- So an empty string would fall through to the ELSE branch: `CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)` - which would give `''::DOUBLE PRECISION` and fail.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 11, 1, 636201, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

Errors:
overall_status: partial
- contact: Database Error in model contact (models/contact.sql)
  syntax error at or near "DE"
  LINE 28: ... WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('DE', 'GERMAN', 'D) THEN 'DE'
                                                                       ^
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/contact.sql
- opportunity: Database Error in model opportunity (models/opportunity.sql)
  invalid input syntax for type double precision: ""
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/opportunity.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 11, 1, 636209, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 11, 1, 636491, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62ae-f783-70c0-9d9a-f2bd3cdb7f9e', conversation_id='019f62ae-f783-70c0-9d9a-f2bc1693a26b')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='The user says dbt run failed with two errors:

1. `contact` model - syntax error at or near "DE" on LINE 28. It seems like there's a quoting issue in the SQL where 'D' appears to be cut off. The line shows `'D)` which suggests a single quote was not properly closed or escaped somewhere, causing the parser to misinterpret the string literal.

2. `opportunity` model - invalid input syntax for type double precision: "". This means some field is trying to cast an empty string to double precision.

Let me first read both failing models to see what's wrong.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/contact.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_y2n964qe'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/opportunity.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_vbg759rd')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=1691, output_tokens=187), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 11, 16, 22335, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 11, 16, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-ef09ee14-972b-4aca-b7ba-21d25cde7b92', finish_reason='tool_call', run_id='019f62ae-f783-70c0-9d9a-f2bd3cdb7f9e', conversation_id='019f62ae-f783-70c0-9d9a-f2bc1693a26b')

ModelRequest(parts=[ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    CONCAT('003', LEFT(MD5(ap_id), 17)) AS "Id",
    INITCAP(TRIM(vorname)) AS "FirstName",
    INITCAP(TRIM(nachname)) AS "LastName",
    LOWER(TRIM(email_adresse)) AS "Email",

    INITCAP(TRIM(position)) AS "Title",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(funktion)) IN ('ENTSCHEIDUNGSBERECHTIGTER', 'DECISION MAKER', 'LEITUNG', 'GESCHÄFTSFÜHRER') THEN 'Decision Maker'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(funktion)) IN ('VORSITZENDER', 'CEO', 'CFO', 'CTO', 'GM', 'GENERAL MANAGER') THEN 'Executive Sponsor'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(funktion)) IN ('TECHNISCHER KONTAKT', 'TECHNICAL CONTACT', 'INGENIEUR', 'IT') THEN 'Technical Contact'
        ELSE 'End User'
    END AS "Role__c",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('DE', 'GERMAN', 'D) THEN 'DE'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('EN', 'ENGLISH') THEN 'EN'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('FR', 'FRENCH') THEN 'FR'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('ES', 'SPANISH') THEN 'ES'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('IT', 'ITALIAN') THEN 'IT'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Preferred_Language__c",
    CONCAT('001', LEFT(MD5(kunde), 17)) AS "AccountId",
    ap_id AS "Legacy_Contact_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'ansprechpartner') }}
', tool_call_id='call_y2n964qe', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 11, 16, 24641, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    CONCAT('006', LEFT(MD5(chance_id), 17)) AS "Id",
    INITCAP(TRIM(bezeichnung)) AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROSPEKT%' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%QUALIFIZIERUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%QUALIFICAT%' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%BEDARFS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEEDS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WERT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VALUE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSITION%' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ENTSCHEID%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%DECISION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%MASTERS%' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WAHRNEHMUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PERCEPTION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANGEBOT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSAL%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PREIS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRICE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ZITAT%' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERHANDLUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEGOTIAT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRÜFUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%REVIEW%' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS GEWONN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED WON%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%GEWONNEN%' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS VERLOREN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED LOST%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERLOREN%' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN waehrung ~ '^[.,\d-]+$' THEN CAST(waehrung AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,.]+', '', 'g'), '^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$', '\1\2.\3')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND NOT waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.]+' , '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ ',' AND NOT waehrung ~ '\.' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,]+', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('USD', 'EURO', 'EUR', '€', '$') THEN 'USD'
        ELSE LEFT(UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)), 3)
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    CONCAT('001', LEFT(MD5(kd_nr), 17)) AS "AccountId",
    chance_id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'chancen') }}

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='Let me analyze the errors:

1. **contact model**: Syntax error at or near "DE" on LINE 28. Looking at the code:
```sql
WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('DE', 'GERMAN', 'D) THEN 'DE'
```
There's a missing closing single quote after `'D`. It should be `'DE'` not `'D)`. This is clearly a typo - the string `'DE'` got cut off.

2. **opportunity model**: invalid input syntax for type double precision: "". This means `waehrung` can be an empty string, and when it goes through the CASE statement, the empty string doesn't match any of the patterns (like `'^[.,\d-]+$'`) properly, or it falls through to a branch where it tries to cast an empty string.

Let me look more carefully at the opportunity model's Amount logic:
- `WHEN waehrung ~ '^[.,\d-]+$'` - this pattern matches strings with only dots, commas, digits, and dashes. An empty string would NOT match this because of the `+` which requires at least one character.
- So an empty string would fall through to the ELSE branch: `CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)` - which would give `''::DOUBLE PRECISION` and fail.

I need to handle empty strings in the opportunity model. Let me add a check for empty strings or NULLs first.

Let me also look at the contact model again:
```sql
WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('DE', 'GERMAN', 'D) THEN 'DE'
```
This should be:
```sql
WHEN UPPER(TRIM(sprache)) IN ('DE', 'GERMAN') THEN 'DE'
```

Now let me also check if there are any other issues. Let me fix both models.

For the contact model, I just need to fix the typo on line 28.

For the opportunity model, I need to add handling for empty strings in the Amount calculation. I should handle the case where waehrung is NULL or empty string.

Let me also consider: if `waehrung` is an empty string, REGEXP_REPLACE would return empty string, and casting '' to DOUBLE PRECISION fails.

I'll fix both issues now.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content='I see two issues:

1. **contact**: Missing closing quote in `'DE'` — it reads `'D)` which breaks the SQL syntax.
2. **opportunity**: Empty string `""` for `waehrung` falls through all CASE branches and gets cast to DOUBLE PRECISION, causing a parse error.

Let me fix both models.'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/contact.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_i9le5gnv'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/opportunity.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_tos4lmv9')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=3275, output_tokens=648), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 11, 38, 339963, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 11, 38, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-2dfb557c-6337-42cf-baf9-416b6efe9c25', finish_reason='tool_call', run_id='019f62ae-f783-70c0-9d9a-f2bd3cdb7f9e', conversation_id='019f62ae-f783-70c0-9d9a-f2bc1693a26b')

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ENTSCHEID%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%DECISION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%MASTERS%' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WAHRNEHMUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PERCEPTION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANGEBOT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSAL%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PREIS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRICE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ZITAT%' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERHANDLUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEGOTIAT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRÜFUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%REVIEW%' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS GEWONN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED WON%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%GEWONNEN%' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS VERLOREN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED LOST%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERLOREN%' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN waehrung ~ '^[.,\d-]+$' THEN CAST(waehrung AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,.]+', '', 'g'), '^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$', '\1\2.\3')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND NOT waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.]+' , '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ ',' AND NOT waehrung ~ '\.' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,]+', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('USD', 'EURO', 'EUR', '€', '$') THEN 'USD'
        ELSE LEFT(UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)), 3)
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    CONCAT('001', LEFT(MD5(kd_nr), 17)) AS "AccountId",
    chance_id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'chancen') }}

messages.raw:
ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
