ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253343, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

Errors:
overall_status: partial

  invalid input syntax for type double precision: ""
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/opportunity.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253354, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253704, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62b3-5334-71f3-9e05-d746df80d3c0', conversation_id='019f62b3-5333-72a1-9dc7-2886be9d947a')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253343, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

Errors:
overall_status: partial
- opportunity: Database Error in model opportunity (models/opportunity.sql)
  invalid input syntax for type double precision: ""
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/opportunity.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253354, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253704, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62b3-5334-71f3-9e05-d746df80d3c0', conversation_id='019f62b3-5333-72a1-9dc7-2886be9d947a')

ModelRequest(parts=[ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    CONCAT('006', LEFT(MD5(chance_id), 17)) AS "Id",
    INITCAP(TRIM(bezeichnung)) AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROSPEKT%' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%QUALIFIZIERUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%QUALIFICAT%' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%BEDARFS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEEDS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WERT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VALUE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSITION%' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ENTSCHEID%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%DECISION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%MASTERS%' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WAHRNEHMUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PERCEPTION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANGEBOT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSAL%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PREIS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRICE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ZITAT%' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERHANDLUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEGOTIAT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRÜFUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%REVIEW%' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS GEWONN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED WON%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%GEWONNEN%' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS VERLOREN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED LOST%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERLOREN%' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN waehrung IS NULL OR TRIM(waehrung) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN waehrung ~ '^[.,\d-]+$' THEN CAST(waehrung AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,.]+', '', 'g'), '^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$', '\1\2.\3')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND NOT waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.]+' , '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ ',' AND NOT waehrung ~ '\.' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,]+', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('USD', 'EURO', 'EUR', '€', '$') THEN 'USD'
        ELSE LEFT(UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)), 3)
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    CONCAT('001', LEFT(MD5(kd_nr), 17)) AS "AccountId",
    chance_id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'chancen') }}
', tool_call_id='call_3op463rk', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 59, 647106, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 59, 647530, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62b3-5334-71f3-9e05-d746df80d3c0', conversation_id='019f62b3-5333-72a1-9dc7-2886be9d947a')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='The error is about an empty string being passed to double precision. Looking at the Amount column logic, there's a case where `waehrung` could be an empty string after regex replacement, or the final ELSE clause might try to cast an empty string to DOUBLE PRECISION.

Let me also check the sources.yml and any other files to understand the full context better. But first, let me fix the Amount column logic. The issue is that the CASE statement has cases that could result in an empty string being cast to double precision, or `waehrung` itself might be just whitespace/empty.

The safest approach:
1. First check if waehrung is NULL or empty after trimming
2. Use REGEXP_REPLACE to extract only numeric chars and decimal point/comma
3. Handle European format (dot as thousands, comma as decimal)
4. Handle US format (comma as thousands, dot as decimal)
5. Cast to DOUBLE PRECISION
6. Add a final guard: if the cleaned value is empty or NULL after all processing, return NULL

Let me look at the source data first to understand what values we're dealing with. But actually, I should just fix the logic to handle empty strings safely.

The problem seems to be in one of these branches:
- `ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)` - if after removing non-numeric chars you get an empty string or just a dot/comma/dash, casting fails.

Let me rewrite this more carefully:

```sql
CASE
    WHEN waehrung IS NULL OR TRIM(waehrung) = '' THEN NULL
    ELSE
        CASE
            WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND waehrung ~ ',' THEN
                -- European format: dots are thousands separators, comma is decimal
                CAST(
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(waehrung), '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g'), '^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$', '\1\2.\3')
                AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
            WHEN waehrung ~ ',' AND NOT waehrung ~ '\.' THEN
                -- US format with comma thousands separator OR comma as decimal (no dot)
                CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(waehrung), '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
            WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND NOT waehrung ~ ',' THEN
                -- US format: dots are separators, no comma
                -- Could be like "1.234,56" or "1234.56" - but we know it has dot and no comma
                -- Actually this means just dots, so treat as thousands separator? Or decimal?
                -- Safest: if it looks like X.XXX (thousands), remove dots; otherwise keep as decimal
                CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(waehrung), '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
            ELSE
                CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(waehrung), '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        END
END
```

Hmm, but the issue is that even after all this, we might get an empty string. Let me add a safer approach:

Actually, let me first check what source data looks like to understand the patterns better.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content='Let me inspect the source data to understand the `waehrung` values causing empty-string-to-double issues.'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='run_dbt', args='{}', tool_call_id='call_nt64dicm'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='query_postgres', args='{"sql": "SELECT waehrung, TRIM(waehrung) as trimmed, LENGTH(TRIM(waehrung)) as len FROM \"fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src\".\"chancen\" LIMIT 30"}', tool_call_id='call_4c7fun83')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=2664, output_tokens=850), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 16, 25, 218579, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 16, 25, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-63158d7d-a1d4-46ec-a6c3-a8726d4dad9c', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253343, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

Errors:
overall_status: partial
- opportunity: Database Error in model opportunity (models/opportunity.sql)
  invalid input syntax for type double precision: ""
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/opportunity.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253354, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253704, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62b3-5334-71f3-9e05-d746df80d3c0', conversation_id='019f62b3-5333-72a1-9dc7-2886be9d947a')

SELECT

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WERT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VALUE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSITION%' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ENTSCHEID%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%DECISION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%MASTERS%' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WAHRNEHMUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PERCEPTION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANGEBOT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSAL%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PREIS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRICE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ZITAT%' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERHANDLUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEGOTIAT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRÜFUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%REVIEW%' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS GEWONN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED WON%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%GEWONNEN%' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS VERLOREN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED LOST%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERLOREN%' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN waehrung IS NULL OR TRIM(waehrung) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN waehrung ~ '^[.,\d-]+$' THEN CAST(waehrung AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,.]+', '', 'g'), '^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$', '\1\2.\3')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND NOT waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.]+' , '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ ',' AND NOT waehrung ~ '\.' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,]+', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('USD', 'EURO', 'EUR', '€', '$') THEN 'USD'
        ELSE LEFT(UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)), 3)
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    CONCAT('001', LEFT(MD5(kd_nr), 17)) AS "AccountId",
    chance_id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'chancen') }}

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253343, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

Errors:
overall_status: partial
- opportunity: Database Error in model opportunity (models/opportunity.sql)
  invalid input syntax for type double precision: ""
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/opportunity.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253354, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253704, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62b3-5334-71f3-9e05-d746df80d3c0', conversation_id='019f62b3-5333-72a1-9dc7-2886be9d947a')

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%BEDARFS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEEDS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Needs Analysis'

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ENTSCHEID%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%DECISION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%MASTERS%' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANGEBOT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSAL%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PREIS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRICE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ZITAT%' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERHANDLUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEGOTIAT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRÜFUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%REVIEW%' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS GEWONN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED WON%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%GEWONNEN%' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS VERLOREN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED LOST%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERLOREN%' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN waehrung IS NULL OR TRIM(waehrung) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN waehrung ~ '^[.,\d-]+$' THEN CAST(waehrung AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,.]+', '', 'g'), '^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$', '\1\2.\3')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND NOT waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.]+' , '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ ',' AND NOT waehrung ~ '\.' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,]+', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('USD', 'EURO', 'EUR', '€', '$') THEN 'USD'
        ELSE LEFT(UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)), 3)
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    CONCAT('001', LEFT(MD5(kd_nr), 17)) AS "AccountId",
    chance_id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'chancen') }}
', tool_call_id='call_3op463rk', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 59, 647106, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 59, 647530, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62b3-5334-71f3-9e05-d746df80d3c0', conversation_id='019f62b3-5333-72a1-9dc7-2886be9d947a')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253343, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

Errors:
overall_status: partial
- opportunity: Database Error in model opportunity (models/opportunity.sql)
  invalid input syntax for type double precision: ""
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/opportunity.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253354, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253704, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62b3-5334-71f3-9e05-d746df80d3c0', conversation_id='019f62b3-5333-72a1-9dc7-2886be9d947a')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content="The error is about an empty string being passed to a double precision column. I need to read the opportunity model and fix the casting logic for amount/currency columns. Let me first read the file to see what's happening.", id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/opportunity.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_3op463rk')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=1619, output_tokens=75), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 59, 644805, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 59, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-05ee407d-8609-4a89-968a-5c542c9f63a2', finish_reason='tool_call', run_id='019f62b3-5334-71f3-9e05-d746df80d3c0', conversation_id='019f62b3-5333-72a1-9dc7-2886be9d947a')

ModelRequest(parts=[ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    CONCAT('006', LEFT(MD5(chance_id), 17)) AS "Id",
    INITCAP(TRIM(bezeichnung)) AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROSPEKT%' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%QUALIFIZIERUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%QUALIFICAT%' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%BEDARFS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEEDS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WERT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VALUE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSITION%' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ENTSCHEID%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%DECISION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%MASTERS%' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WAHRNEHMUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PERCEPTION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANGEBOT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSAL%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PREIS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRICE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ZITAT%' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERHANDLUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEGOTIAT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRÜFUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%REVIEW%' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS GEWONN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED WON%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%GEWONNEN%' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS VERLOREN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED LOST%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERLOREN%' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN waehrung IS NULL OR TRIM(waehrung) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN waehrung ~ '^[.,\d-]+$' THEN CAST(waehrung AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,.]+', '', 'g'), '^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$', '\1\2.\3')::DOUBLE PRECISION

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='Now I understand the full context. The issue is in the `opportunity` model's `Amount` column. The error is:

                AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
            ELSE
                CAST(
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(waehrung), '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g'), '^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$', '\1\2.\3')
                AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        END
END
```

Wait, but the European format regex `'^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$'` might not match all European formats. Let me think more carefully...

Actually, the issue is simpler than I'm making it. The core problem: some `waehrung` values (which represents currency amount in German source) contain only non-numeric characters like "$$$", "abc", etc. After stripping non-numeric characters with `REGEXP_REPLACE(..., '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g')`, you get an empty string or just a dot/comma/dash, and then casting that to DOUBLE PRECISION fails.

The simplest fix is to add a guard: if the cleaned value (after removing non-numeric chars) has no digits at all, return NULL. Also handle cases where after cleaning, we might have only dots/commas without digits.

Let me also be careful about the European number format detection. The safest approach for amounts:

1. Strip non-numeric characters (keeping digits, dots, commas, minus signs)
2. If no digits remain → NULL
3. If both dot and comma present → assume European format (last comma is decimal point, dots are thousands)
4. If only commas → could be US thousands separator or European decimal  
5. If only dots → could be thousands separator or decimal

For simplicity and robustness, I'll use this approach:

```sql
CASE
    WHEN waehrung IS NULL OR TRIM(waehrung) = '' THEN NULL
    ELSE
        CAST(
            CASE 
                -- Both dot and comma: European format (dot=thousands, comma=decimal)
                WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND waehrung ~ ',' THEN
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g'), ',', '.', 1, -1) -- Replace last comma with dot
                -- Only comma: European decimal format
                WHEN waehrung ~ ',' AND NOT waehrung ~ '\.' THEN
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g')
                -- Only dots or mixed: remove all non-numeric except digits and last dot
                ELSE REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g')
            END
        AS DOUBLE PRECISION)

```

Hmm, but PostgreSQL's `REGEXP_REPLACE` doesn't have a straightforward "replace last occurrence" option. Let me think differently.

Actually, the simplest and most robust approach: remove all dots and commas except the last comma/dot to handle European formats:

```sql
-- For amounts with both dot and comma (European): replace dots with nothing (remove thousands separators), then replace last comma with decimal point
REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g'), '\.', '')  -- remove thousand separator dots

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253343, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

Errors:
overall_status: partial
- opportunity: Database Error in model opportunity (models/opportunity.sql)
  invalid input syntax for type double precision: ""
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/opportunity.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253354, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 15, 47, 253704, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62b3-5334-71f3-9e05-d746df80d3c0', conversation_id='019f62b3-5333-72a1-9dc7-2886be9d947a')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.

ModelRequest(parts=[ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    CONCAT('006', LEFT(MD5(chance_id), 17)) AS "Id",
    INITCAP(TRIM(bezeichnung)) AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROSPEKT%' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%QUALIFIZIERUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%QUALIFICAT%' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%BEDARFS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEEDS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WERT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VALUE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSITION%' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ENTSCHEID%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%DECISION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%MASTERS%' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%WAHRNEHMUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PERCEPTION%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANALYSE%' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ANGEBOT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PROPOSAL%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PREIS%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRICE%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ZITAT%' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERHANDLUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEGOTIAT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRÜFUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%REVIEW%' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS GEWONN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED WON%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%GEWONNEN%' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS VERLOREN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED LOST%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERLOREN%' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN waehrung IS NULL OR TRIM(waehrung) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN waehrung ~ '^[.,\d-]+$' THEN CAST(waehrung AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,.]+', '', 'g'), '^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$', '\1\2.\3')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND NOT waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.]+' , '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ ',' AND NOT waehrung ~ '\.' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,]+', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('USD', 'EURO', 'EUR', '€', '$') THEN 'USD'
        ELSE LEFT(UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)), 3)
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    CONCAT('001', LEFT(MD5(kd_nr), 17)) AS "AccountId",
    chance_id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'chancen') }}
', tool_call_id='call_3op463rk', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 20, 4, 492709, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 20, 4, 493147, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62b7-2c42-7206-8a7c-59b3603165b2', conversation_id='019f62b7-2c42-7206-8a7c-59b20ce43562')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='The error is about an empty string being passed to double precision. Looking at the Amount column logic, there's a case where `waehrung` could be an empty string after regex replacement, or the final ELSE clause might try to cast an empty string to DOUBLE PRECISION.

Let me also check the sources.yml and any other files to understand the full context better. But first, let me fix the Amount column logic. The issue is that the CASE statement has cases that could result in an empty string being cast to double precision, or `waehrung` itself might be just whitespace/empty.

The safest approach:
1. First check if waehrung is NULL or empty after trimming
2. Use REGEXP_REPLACE to extract only numeric chars and decimal point/comma
3. Handle European format (dot as thousands, comma as decimal)
4. Handle US format (comma as thousands, dot as decimal)
5. Cast to DOUBLE PRECISION
6. Add a final guard: if the cleaned value is empty or NULL after all processing, return NULL

Let me look at the source data first to understand what values we're dealing with. But actually, I should just fix the logic to handle empty strings safely.

The problem seems to be in one of these branches:
- `ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)` - if after removing non-numeric chars you get an empty string or just a dot/comma/dash, casting fails.

Let me rewrite this more carefully:

```sql
CASE
    WHEN waehrung IS NULL OR TRIM(waehrung) = '' THEN NULL
    ELSE
        CASE
            WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND waehrung ~ ',' THEN
                -- European format: dots are thousands separators, comma is decimal
                CAST(
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(waehrung), '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g'), '^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$', '\1\2.\3')
                AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
            WHEN waehrung ~ ',' AND NOT waehrung ~ '\.' THEN
                -- US format with comma thousands separator OR comma as decimal (no dot)
                CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(waehrung), '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
            WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND NOT waehrung ~ ',' THEN
                -- US format: dots are separators, no comma
                -- Could be like "1.234,56" or "1234.56" - but we know it has dot and no comma
                -- Actually this means just dots, so treat as thousands separator? Or decimal?
                -- Safest: if it looks like X.XXX (thousands), remove dots; otherwise keep as decimal
                CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(waehrung), '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
            ELSE
                CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(waehrung), '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        END
END
```

Hmm, but the issue is that even after all this, we might get an empty string. Let me add a safer approach:

Actually, let me first check what source data looks like to understand the patterns better.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content='Let me inspect the source data to understand the `waehrung` values causing empty-string-to-double issues.'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='run_dbt', args='{}', tool_call_id='call_nt64dicm'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='query_postgres', args='{"sql": "SELECT waehrung, TRIM(waehrung) as trimmed, LENGTH(TRIM(waehrung)) as len FROM \"fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src\".\"chancen\" LIMIT 30"}', tool_call_id='call_4c7fun83')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=2664, output_tokens=850), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 20, 9, 567898, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 16, 25, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-63158d7d-a1d4-46ec-a6c3-a8726d4dad9c', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 19, 59, 427865, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

Errors:

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 19, 59, 427865, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

Errors:
overall_status: partial
- opportunity: Database Error in model opportunity (models/opportunity.sql)
  invalid input syntax for type double precision: ""
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/opportunity.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 19, 59, 427874, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 19, 59, 428183, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62b7-2c42-7206-8a7c-59b3603165b2', conversation_id='019f62b7-2c42-7206-8a7c-59b20ce43562')

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERHANDLUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%NEGOTIAT%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%PRÜFUNG%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%REVIEW%' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS GEWONN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED WON%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%GEWONNEN%' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%ABSCHLUSS VERLOREN%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%CLOSED LOST%' OR UPPER(TRIM(phase)) LIKE '%VERLOREN%' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN abschlussdatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(abschlussdatum, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN waehrung IS NULL OR TRIM(waehrung) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN waehrung ~ '^[.,\d-]+$' THEN CAST(waehrung AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,.]+', '', 'g'), '^([0-9]*)\.([0-9]{3})[,]([0-9]+)$', '\1\2.\3')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ '\.' AND NOT waehrung ~ ',' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.]+' , '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN waehrung ~ ',' AND NOT waehrung ~ '\.' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9,]+', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(waehrung, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('USD', 'EURO', 'EUR', '€', '$') THEN 'USD'
        ELSE LEFT(UPPER(TRIM(waehrung)), 3)
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    CONCAT('001', LEFT(MD5(kd_nr), 17)) AS "AccountId",
    chance_id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'chancen') }}
', tool_call_id='call_3op463rk', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 20, 4, 492709, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 20, 4, 493147, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62b7-2c42-7206-8a7c-59b3603165b2', conversation_id='019f62b7-2c42-7206-8a7c-59b20ce43562')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
